details.prompt: you are a world class mathematician orchestrating a team to solve olympiad math problems. read claude.md for instructions and solve the problem imo-2026-04.

details.text: I'll start by reading run state and the problem's workspace.

Fresh run. The user explicitly named `imo-2026-04` (a triangle-cutting game — find all θ for which Mulan can force a win), which overrides the "hard only" default. Setting up environment and run state.
Dispatching three math-explorers in parallel, each scouting a genuinely different route to the problem.

details.final_text:
Report written to /tmp/round-1/math-explorer-retrieval.md.

Summary: The most transferable technique found is the branch-splitting invariant from aimo-0917 (IMO SL 2020 C8) — a genuine mechanical analog where an adversary picks one of two proposed successor states, and the crux move is forcing a favorable branch via an invariant split N=N₊+N₋. I also derived (by direct computation, not conjecture) that the cut mechanics reduce each move to producing children {B,α1,C+α2} and {C,α2,B+α1}, and proved that the naive "always subtract θ from the max angle" strategy alone can never force a win for any θ, since it only shifts each angle by integer multiples of θ, letting Shan-Yu pick an initial triangle avoiding all θ-residues — a genuine dead end to flag. aimo-0355 (quirky triangles, USA TSTST 2020) offers the right style of rational/commensurability argument for pinning down the exact characterization of winning θ, since no true geometry-domain crux exists in the corpus yet.

Report written to /tmp/round-1/math-explorer-invariants.md

Summary: The cut splits the attacked vertex's angle R into r1+r2, giving children (P,r1,Q+r2) and (Q,r2,P+r1) with Shan-Yu choosing the survivor; I proved rigorously that for θ>90° Shan-Yu wins forever via the invariant "all angles < θ" (unbreakable exactly when 2θ>180), and that for θ=90° Mulan wins in exactly one universal move (since a triangle has at most one angle ≥90°, forcing both children to contain 90° via r1=Q+R−θ). For θ<90° I found a promising but incomplete "create angle 2θ then halve it" resonance construction (clean at θ=60°,45°, general case open) plus two identified dead-end naive strategies (period-3 and period-2 cycles) that the outliner should avoid retrying. My best-guess answer is **Mulan wins iff 0°<θ≤90°**, rigorous on the θ≥90° side, conjectural-but-well-supported on the θ<90° side.

Report written to /tmp/round-1/math-explorer-simulation.md

Summary: I derived the exact cut model (a cevian from the opposite vertex splits that vertex's angle α into α₁+α₂, giving children (β,α₁,180−β−α₁) and (γ,α−α₁,180−γ−α+α₁)), and proved analytically that the only single-move "double threat" mechanisms are (A) an existing angle = 2θ (split it in half) requiring θ<90°, and (B) θ=90° exactly, which is a universal one-move win from any starting triangle. Value-iteration simulation (grids at 1° and 0.5° resolution, depth up to 10) strongly and robustly shows θ>90° is permanently stuck (Shan-Yu escapes forever) while essentially all tested θ≤90° eventually reach a full win once resolution/depth are large enough (many earlier "stuck" cases at coarse resolution, e.g. 55°, 85°, 89°, turned out to be rounding artifacts, not real obstructions). My conjectured answer is **Mulan wins iff θ ∈ (0°, 90°]**, with high confidence on the θ>90° impossibility and medium confidence on θ<90° always winning (boundary values near 89.5°/90.5° showed an unresolved numeric anomaly flagged for analytic follow-up).

Report written to /tmp/round-1/proof-outliner.md

Field of approaches (files in /home/agentuser/repo/results/imo-2026-04/approaches/):

- **residue-divisibility-characterization** (new, primary) — corrected answer: Mulan wins iff 180/θ ∈ ℤ; Shan-Yu mod-θ residue invariant for necessity, fork-to-multiples + downward induction on kθ for sufficiency; gaps are write-up-level only.
- **circle-group-quotient** (new, rival framing, same answer) — project the game to ℝ/θℤ, decide by σ = [180] = 0, handle all size constraints in one separate lifting lemma.
- **escalation-below-90** (new, falsification rival) — the explorers' original θ ≤ 90 answer, kept alive solely to audit the residue invariant; gated on finding a hole, expected to be recorded as refuted.

Note for the orchestrator: the explorers' conjecture θ ≤ 90 is overturned — the mod-θ residue argument plus exact-fraction adversarial search (/tmp/exact_check.py: wins at θ = 30, 36, 45, 60; no win at θ = 40, 55) point to the answer **θ = 180°/n, n ≥ 2**; the round-1 grid simulations were rounding artifacts.

Report written to /tmp/round-1/outline-reviewer.md

**Answer adjudication (the key call this round):** the two rival conjectures were tested with exact-fraction arithmetic (`/tmp/verify_review.py`). The "θ ≤ 90°" answer is **refuted**: at θ = 80, 55, 40, 79, from 2000 random θℤ-free triples, every structured Mulan cut (all four residue-class representatives, all three vertices) left Shan-Yu a θℤ-free child — zero invariant violations. Conversely the fork + Lemma-A strategy won from ~300 random starts each at θ = 180/n for n = 2..9, zero failures (including the n = 2 Lemma D fallback, verified: r2 = 90−Q is always in range since R − r2 = 90 − P > 0). The cut model (vertex, t) is complete against the exact statement wording, closing escalation-below-90's last escape hatch. The field builds toward **Mulan wins iff 180/θ ∈ ℤ**.

**Verdicts and ranking:**
- `residue-divisibility-characterization` — **APPROVE**, Elo 1516 (leader). All three lemma mechanisms independently verified; GAPs A–C are write-up level. Advised: for n = 2, route sufficiency entirely through Lemma D rather than patching the fork's t₀ < R bound.
- `circle-group-quotient` — **APPROVE**, Elo 1484. Honest flag: it shares its mathematical core with the leader (framing rival, not deep-strategy rival) — acceptable only because the core is now verified; must keep its algebra/lifting layer separation or it's a worse copy.
- `escalation-below-90` — **RETHINK / cut, not registered.** All three gate conditions fail; its own Step 3 re-derives the rival's invariant against itself. Record "θ ≤ 90" as a dead end in `current.md`.

Diversity note for the orchestrator: the surviving field is two framings of one verified idea — fine while gaps are bookkeeping, but if a builder stalls structurally, next round needs a genuinely different framing.

build set: residue-divisibility-characterization, circle-group-quotient

Proof written to /home/agentuser/repo/results/imo-2026-04/approaches/residue-divisibility-characterization.md (Status: solved)

All three gaps (A, B, C) are closed: Lemma 2's induction is written in full, the n = 2 case is routed entirely through the one-cut Lemma 3 per the reviewer's advice (so the fork's t₀ < R bound is only claimed for n ≥ 3 where it is strict), and the necessity invariant is a single four-case argument over ℝ/θℤ with the relabeling symmetry made explicit — plus the cut model is derived from the statement in both directions (form + IVT achievability). Final answer stated and verified: Mulan wins iff θ = 180°/n for an integer n ≥ 2, with an explicit bound of at most n − 1 cuts; build report at /tmp/round-1/proof-builder-residue.md; no remaining gaps.

Proof written to /home/agentuser/repo/results/imo-2026-04/approaches/circle-group-quotient.md (Status: solved). The layered proof is complete with no remaining gaps: cut-model bijection (Lemma 0, Crossbar Theorem), size-free quotient dichotomy in ℝ/θℤ (Lemmas 2A/2B), necessity via a clean starting triple plus Shan-Yu closure, and sufficiency via Lift 1 (t₀ ∈ (0, θ) < 60 ≤ R for n ≥ 3), Lift 2 (kθ ⟹ win in ≤ k−1 cuts, full induction), and the self-contained θ = 90° one-move win for n = 2 — final answer stated: Mulan wins iff θ = 180°/n, n ≥ 2, within ≤ n−1 cuts. Build report at /tmp/round-1/proof-builder-circle.md, which also flags promotable lemmas L0/L1/L2 for the reviewer to certify and records an exact-fraction verbatim-strategy verification (n = 2–18, zero failures).

Review written to /tmp/round-1/proof-reviewer.md.

- **residue-divisibility-characterization — APPROVE (solved):** every load-bearing step re-derived independently (cut model checked with exact coordinate geometry; four-case invariant confirmed exhaustive given parent cleanness and verified by exact-fraction search at θ = 55, 80, 100, 170, 179, 270/7; strategy simulated against exhaustive Shan-Yu keeps for n = 2..12 with worst case exactly n−1 cuts); only a trivial presentational hair in Lemma 2's step.
- **circle-group-quotient — APPROVE (solved):** same verified mathematics with the cleanest write-up (early-stop case handled explicitly, layered quotient/lift structure airtight) — certified as the Full proof in `/home/agentuser/repo/results/imo-2026-04/current.md`, with the residue proof recorded as independent verification; all four shared lemmas (L0–L3) certified into `/home/agentuser/repo/results/imo-2026-04/lemmas/`.

Goal Progress: SOLVED — imo-2026-04 fully proven (Mulan wins iff θ = 180°/n, integer n ≥ 2, within ≤ n−1 cuts), two independent verified proofs; run goal met in round 1.

Your lens: **exact game-dynamics and invariants**. Analyze what one cut does to the angle triple: if T has angles (α, β, γ) and Mulan cuts from a point on the side opposite vertex with angle γ, the vertex angle γ is split as γ = γ₁ + γ₂ (Mulan chooses the split continuously in (0,γ)), and the two pieces have angle triples (α, γ₁, 180−α−γ₁) and (β, γ₂, 180−β−γ₂); Shan-Yu picks which survives. # Crux corpus — files, schema & subtopics

The crux corpus is a retrieval bank of the load-bearing moves of solved pre-2026
Olympiad problems (disjoint from the 2026 test set), stored as two JSON arrays
joined by `problem_id`:

- `past_crux_moves_database.json` — the load-bearing move of each solved pre-2026
  problem. **2434 cruxes** across three domains: number_theory (548),
  combinatorics (1114), algebra (772).
- `past_problems_database.json` — the full statement + solution(s) of those
  problems (1026 problems; covers every `problem_id` referenced by a crux).

**Use the exact field names below. Do not guess** — `crux_move` and `statement` do
**not** exist and querying them returns blank content.

## `past_crux_moves_database.json` fields

| field | meaning |
|---|---|
| `problem_id` | join key |
| `technique` | the crux move — the name of the load-bearing idea |
| `how_used` | how that move cracked the problem |
| `domain` | one of `number_theory`, `combinatorics`, `algebra`; use this to filter first |
| `subtopic` | one subtopic (see list below); use this to filter within a domain |
| `technique_tags`, `subtopics`, `retries` | secondary metadata |

## `past_problems_database.json` fields

| field | meaning |
|---|---|
| `problem_id` | join key |
| `problem` | the full statement (**not** `statement` / `problem_statement`) |
| `solutions` | the full solution(s) |
| `domain`, `competition`, `exam`, `year`, `problem_label`, `problem_type` | metadata |

## Subtopics by domain

Each crux carries one `domain` and one `subtopic`. Filter by `domain` first, then
by these `subtopic` values (listed most-common first). Match the strings exactly.

# Olympiad Knowledge Base

Theorems and techniques for hard (difficulty-8) olympiad problems across algebra,
number theory, combinatorics, geometry, and linear algebra. Organized by technique,
not by problem domain — many problems mix areas.

## Algebra & Polynomials

- **Vieta's formulas**: roots ↔ coefficients. For systems of symmetric equations,
  treat unknowns as roots of a polynomial and read off symmetric functions.
- **Symmetric functions & Newton's identities**: convert between power sums
  `p_k = Σ xᵢᵏ` and elementary symmetric polynomials. Use to match power-sum equalities.
- **Resultants / "transform the roots"**: to show `P(r)=0 ⟹ Q(f(r))=0`, exhibit a
  polynomial identity `g(x)·P(x) = Q(f(x))`; then `P(r)=0` forces `Q(f(r))=0`.
- **Minimal-polynomial reduction**: given `P(x)=0`, reduce any high power `xᵏ` by
  repeatedly substituting the relation (e.g. `x³ = 8 − 4x`) until degree `< deg P`.
- **Sum of squares (SOS) / completing the square**: prove a real inequality by
  rewriting `LHS − RHS` as a sum of squares; equivalently a positive-semidefinite
  quadratic form.
- **Piecewise-concavity smoothing**: to minimize a function `g(φ)` that is a finite
  sum of terms each of the form `|single sinusoid|` (i.e. `|A cos(kφ)+B sin(kφ)|`),
  note each such term is concave on every interval between two consecutive zeros of
  its argument (an identically-zero term is constant, hence concave). A finite sum of
  functions concave on a common subinterval is concave there. Partitioning the circle
  by the (finitely many) zeros of all arguments gives a piecewise-concave `g`; a
  concave function on a closed interval attains its min at an endpoint, so the global
  min of `g` is at a breakpoint, where some sinusoid argument vanishes. If the term is
  a product `|x_φ · y|`, vanishing forces a factor to vanish — a "zero entry."
  Moreover, if `φ=0` is already a global minimizer and lies in the interior of a
  concavity interval, concavity forces `g` constant on that interval, so its endpoints
  are also global minimizers carrying an extra zero entry. Used to drive a minimizer to
  a sparse (axial) configuration without raising the objective.
- **Standard inequalities**: AM-GM, Cauchy-Schwarz, QM-AM, Schur. Equality cases
  pin down the extremal configuration.
- **Functional equations**: test special values, check injectivity/surjectivity.
- **Complex roots & moduli**: `|root product| = |constant term|`; conjugate
  symmetry (`b = ā`) and `aⁿ = bⁿ` cases for modulus conditions.

## Linear Algebra

- **Quadratic forms**: a symmetric form is PSD iff it's a sum of squares iff all
  eigenvalues ≥ 0. `det = 0` ⟺ nontrivial kernel.
- **Symmetric matrices**: real symmetric ⟹ orthogonally diagonalizable, real
  eigenvalues. `A²` is PSD; `Σ Aᵢ²` singular ⟺ the `Aᵢ` share a common null vector.
- **Rank / image / kernel**: `det(Σ Aᵢ Bᵢ) = 0 ∀Bᵢ` ⟺ the `Aᵢ` have a common
  nonzero vector in `∩ ker` (or images miss a direction) — argue via shared kernel.
- **Extrem...

## imo-2026-04

### Cut model (verified from statement)
P lies on the side opposite a vertex; cutting P to that vertex splits **that vertex's angle**, not the sides' angles. So if T has angles (P,Q,R) at vertices A,B,C and Mulan cuts from a point on side AB to the opposite vertex C, she splits angle R=∠C into r1+r2=R (r1,r2>0, chosen by Mulan, any reals). The two children are
  T1 = (P, r1, Q+r2),   T2 = (Q, r2, P+r1)

## imo-2026-04 (lens: computational experiment / conjecture-hunting)

### Cut model (derived from the statement)
Triangle has angles (α at A, β at B, γ at C), α+β+γ=180°. "Cut from P on the
perimeter (≠ vertex) to the opposite vertex" means: P lies on one side, say BC,
and the cevian goes to the OPPOSITE vertex A. This is a cevian from A, so it
splits **A's own angle** α into two parts α₁+α₂=α (α₁ = the choice, continuous,
Mulan picks the side ⇒ which vertex's angle gets split, AND the continuous split
ratio t=α₁/α∈(0,1)). The two children are:
- Triangle 1 = (β, α₁, 180−β−α₁)  [B kept whole; new "point angle" at P]
- Triangle 2 = (γ, α−α₁, 180−γ−(α−α₁))
Note the two point-angles at P sum to 180° (angles on a line), consistent check
passed. So Mulan's move = (choice of vertex to split) × (continuous split point).
Shan-Yu then keeps one child. This matches the problem statement exactly (P on a
side, cut to the opposite vertex, Shan-Yu discards one piece).

Solved "for which (α,β,γ,θ) does SOME split of vertex A make BOTH children
contain angle θ instantly" (assuming θ∉{α,β,γ}, i.e. game hasn't already ended).
Four sign combinations of the θ-hit equations reduce to only two non-degenerate
solutions:
- **(A) α = 2θ** (split the vertex with angle exactly 2θ exactly in half:
  α₁=α₂=θ). Requires 2θ<180 i.e. θ<90°, plus mild positivity conditions.
- **(B) θ = 90° exactly**, independent of the triangle's other angles as long as
  the two non-split angles β,γ are both <90° — always arrangeable (only one
  angle of a triangle can be ≥90°, so split at that vertex, or any vertex if the
  triangle is acute). This gives a **universal one-move win at θ=90°** from
  literally any starting triangle not already right-angled.
No other single-move double-threat mechanism exists (the other 2 sign cases force
a degenerate 0° angle). This is a clean structural fact, not just conjecture.

containing θ}; Winₖ = Winₖ₋₁ ∪ {states with some vertex/split t s.t. BOTH children

  split is a discretization artifact, not real**: re-running θ=55, 85, 89 at
  finer resolution (0.5°) shows they are NOT stuck — t...

# Outline review — imo-2026-04, round 1

## Answer-conjecture adjudication (the contradiction the field carried)

Two mutually exclusive answers were on the table: the outliner's **"Mulan wins iff 180/θ ∈ ℤ"** vs the explorers' **"Mulan wins iff θ ≤ 90°"**. I audited both hard, analytically and with exact-fraction computation (`/tmp/verify_review.py`, Fractions, no floats):

Both hit only if 0≡R, 0≡−P, Q≡0, or P+Q+R ≡ 180 ≡ 0 (mod θ) — all excluded when 180/θ ∉ ℤ. The algebra is correct and needs no rationality of θ (works in ℝ/θℤ). The n = 2 fallback (Lemma D: attack the vertex whose other two angles are < 90, r2 = 90−Q; needs r2 < R, which holds since R − r2 = 90 − P > 0) verified with zero failures on 500 acute samples.

## Status
partial

## Approaches tried
- Mod-θ residue invariant extended from forced moves to ALL moves (round 1) — worked as a skeleton; the necessity direction (Shan-Yu survives when θ ∤ 180) and both sufficiency mechanisms (fork-to-multiples + downward induction on kθ) are written below with explicit gaps to formalize.

## Current best
Answer targeted: **Mulan can guarantee victory iff 180/θ is a positive integer**, i.e. θ = 180°/n for an integer n ≥ 2 (θ ∈ {90°, 60°, 45°, 36°, 30°, ...}).

---

## Proof body (with gaps)

**Answer.** Mulan can guarantee victory in finitely many steps exactly when 180/θ ∈ ℤ, i.e. θ = 180°/n for some integer n ≥ 2. (Note 0 < θ < 180 forces n ≥ 2 automatically, so "180/θ ∈ ℤ" is the whole condition; in particular θ ≤ 90° is implied, not assumed.)

**Setup / cut model.** Identify a triangle with its angle triple (P, Q, R), P + Q + R = 180. Cutting from an interior point of the side opposite the vertex carrying R splits R = r1 + r2 (r1, r2 > 0, Mulan's continuous choice), producing children
- C1 = (P, r1, Q + r2) and C2 = (Q, r2, P + r1),
each summing to 180. Shan-Yu keeps one. Mulan also chooses which of the three angles to attack. The game stops (Mulan wins) at the start of a round if the current triple contains θ.

**Step 1 (Lemma A — multiples of θ are winning).** If the current triangle has some angle equal to kθ exactly (k ≥ 1 integer, kθ < 180), Mulan wins in at most k−1 further cuts, whatever Shan-Yu does.
*Mechanism:* induction on k. k = 1: game already stops. k ≥ 2: attack the angle kθ with r1 = θ, r2 = (k−1)θ (valid split: 0 < θ < kθ). C1 = (P, θ, Q + (k−1)θ) contains θ; C2 = (Q, (k−1)θ, P + θ) contains (k−1)θ, winning by the inductive hypothesis. Both children winning ⟹ position winning.
*GAP A (small):* write this rigorously, including the check that a positive angle ≡ 0 (mod θ) is automatically of the form kθ with 1 ≤ k, kθ < 180.

**Step 2 (Sufficiency: θ = 180/n ⟹ Mulan wins from every start).** Let (P, Q, R) be any triangle with no angle already in θℤ (otherwise Step 1 finishes). Let R be the largest angle (so R ≥ 60). Choose t ∈ (0, R) with t ≡ −P (mod θ); the smallest positive representative t₀ of −P mod θ lies in (0, θ) ⊆ (0, 90] and satisfies t₀ < θ ≤ 90 ≤ ... use t = t₀ and verify t₀ < R. Then:
- C2 = (Q, R − t, P + t) has P + t ≡ 0 (mod θ), a positive multiple of θ ⟹ winning by Lemma A;
- C1 = (P, t, Q + R − t) has Q + R − t ≡ Q + R + P ≡ 180 ≡ 0 (mod θ) (using n θ = 180), a positive multiple of θ ⟹ winning by Lemma A.
Both children winning ⟹ Mulan wins, in a number of moves bounded in terms of n only.

## Current best
Same target answer as the field: **Mulan wins iff 180/θ ∈ ℤ.** Distinct framing: separate the game into (i) a purely algebraic quotient game in the group G = ℝ/θℤ, where the answer is decided by whether the constant σ := [180] ∈ G is 0, and (ii) a lifting lemma showing the real (size-constrained) game realizes exactly the quotient game's options. This framing survives even if the direct write-up (residue-divisibility-characterization) stumbles on size/boundary cases, because sizes are handled once, in one lemma.

---

## Proof body (with gaps)

**Answer.** Mulan wins iff 180/θ ∈ ℤ (θ = 180°/n, n ≥ 2).

**Step 1 (the quotient game).** Map a state (P, Q, R) to the multiset π(P,Q,R) = {[P], [Q], [R]} ⊂ G = ℝ/θℤ. A cut attacking R with parameter t maps children to {[P], [t], [Q+R−t]} and {[Q], [R−t], [P+t]}; the element-sum of every reachable state equals σ = [180] ∈ G (check: both children sum to [P]+[Q]+[R]). Winning terminal states project into W₀ = {states containing [0]}, since an angle equal to θ has class [0].
*Mechanism:* direct computation from the cut model; the sum invariant is one line.

**Step 2 (quotient game dichotomy — the algebraic heart).**
- If σ ≠ 0 in G: from any state with no element [0], for every attacked coordinate and every t ∈ ℝ, at least one child again has no element [0]. *Mechanism:* "both children hit [0]" forces [t] ∈ {0, [Q+R]} ∩ {[R], [−P]}, and each of the four intersections implies [P] = 0, [Q] = 0, [R] = 0, or σ = 0 — all excluded. So the set "no element is 0" is Shan-Yu-closed.
- If σ = 0 in G: from any state with no element [0], the choice [t] = [−P] puts [P+t] = 0 in C2 and [Q+R−t] = [Q+R+P] = σ = 0 in C1 — both children contain [0]. One fork suffices in the quotient.

**Step 3 (lifting lemma — from quotient to the real game).** Two directions:
- (Necessity lift) If σ ≠ 0: Shan-Yu picks any initial triangle with no angle in θℤ (finitely many excluded values per coordinate) and, at every move, keeps a child with no angle in θℤ (exists by Step 2). No angle is ever exactly θ; he survives forever. *Mechanism:* the quotient obstruction needs no size information at all — projection only forgets information, so avoiding [0] in G implies avoiding θ in ℝ.

from fractions import Fraction as F
import random
random.seed(7)

def mulan_win(tri, theta, n, depth=0):
    # returns number of cuts needed vs worst-case Shan-Yu, or None if strategy illegal
    P,Q,R = tri
    assert P>0 and Q>0 and R>0 and P+Q+R==180
    if theta in tri: return 0
    mult = [x for x in tri if (x/theta).denominator==1]
    if n==2:
        tri2 = sorted(tri); P,Q,R = tri2[0], tri2[1], tri2[2]
        t = 90 - P
        assert 0 < t < R, tri
        C1=(P,t,Q+R-t); C2=(Q,R-t,P+t)
        assert 90 in C1 and 90 in C2
        return 1
    if mult:
        x = mult[0]; k = int(x/theta); rest=[a for a in tri if a is not x][:2]
        # careful with duplicates: rebuild
        tri_l=list(tri); tri_l.remove(x); A,B=tri_l

A *state* of the game is the current paper triangle, recorded by its (unordered) triple of angles — three positive reals summing to 180. The game protocol, from the statement: Shan-Yu chooses the initial triangle; then, repeatedly, **at the start of each round** the stop-check is performed (if some angle of 𝒯 equals θ exactly, the game stops and Mulan wins); otherwise Mulan chooses a perimeter point distinct from the vertices, cuts to the opposite vertex, and Shan-Yu keeps one of the two resulting triangles. (Note the check also applies to the initial triangle.) Any prescribed triple of positive reals summing to 180 is realizable as a triangle, so Shan-Yu's initial choice is exactly a choice of such a triple.

both nondegenerate triangles (all angles positive). Shan-Yu then keeps C₁ or C₂ at his choice.

*Proof.* A perimeter point X distinct from the vertices lies in the interior of exactly one side; say X ∈ AB (interior), so the opposite vertex is C, and the cut is the segment XC. # Lemma L0 (cut model) — CERTIFIED (round 1)

**Statement.** Let 𝒯 be a triangle with angles P, Q, R at its three vertices. Mulan's legal moves (perimeter point distinct from the vertices, cut to the opposite vertex) are exactly the pairs (attacked vertex with angle R, split parameter t ∈ (0, R)), producing the two nondegenerate children with angle triples
C₁ = (P, t, Q + R − t) and C₂ = (Q, R − t, P + t).
Both directions hold: every legal move has this form, and every such pair is realizable (via the Crossbar Theorem, or IVT on the angle swept from the attacked vertex).

**Proof.** Lemma 0 of `approaches/circle-group-quotient.md` (Crossbar version); Lemma 1 of `approaches/residue-divisibility-characterization.md` (IVT version). Independently verified by the proof-reviewer with exact coordinate geometry on 200 random triangles/cut points.

**Consequence.** The game depends only on the (unordered) angle triple; both the stop condition and both players' option sets are functions of the triple alone.

# Lemma L2 (exact multiple finishes in ≤ k − 1 cuts) — CERTIFIED (round 1)

**Statement.** Let θ = 180°/n, n ≥ 2 an integer. If the current triangle has an angle equal to kθ exactly, for an integer 1 ≤ k ≤ n − 1, then Mulan wins using at most k − 1 further cuts, no matter how Shan-Yu plays. (Strategy: if an angle equals θ the round-start check has already stopped the game; otherwise attack the vertex with angle kθ using t = θ, giving children containing θ and (k−1)θ respectively; descend.)

# Lemma L1 (θ = 90°: universal one-cut win) — CERTIFIED (round 1)

**Statement.** Let θ = 90°. From any triangle with no angle equal to 90°, Mulan has a legal cut after which BOTH children contain an angle of exactly 90°: label R a largest angle and P, Q the others (then P, Q < 90), attack R with t = 90 − P; the children are (P, 90 − P, 90) and (Q, R + P − 90, 90). Hence Mulan wins with at most 1 cut from any start.

# Lemma L3 (cleanness invariant / Shan-Yu closure) — CERTIFIED (round 1)

**Statement.** Suppose 180 ∉ θℤ (equivalently 180/θ ∉ ℤ; θ may be irrational). Call a triple *clean* if none of its three angles lies in θℤ = {mθ : m ∈ ℤ}. If the current triple is clean, then for EVERY legal Mulan move (any attacked vertex, any t in (0, attacked angle)) at least one of the two children (per Lemma L0) is clean.

**Proof.** Step 3b of `approaches/residue-divisibility-characterization.md` = Lemma 2A of `approaches/circle-group-quotient.md`: if both children were dirty then, since P, Q ∉ θℤ, we need (t ≡ 0 or t ≡ Q + R) and (t ≡ R or t ≡ −P) mod θ; each of the four combinations forces R ≡ 0, P ≡ 0, Q ≡ 0, or P + Q + R = 180 ≡ 0 (mod θ) — all contradictions. Independently verified by exact-fraction search at θ = 55, 80, 100, 170, 179, 270/7 over all residue-critical t.

**Consequence.** With a clean initial triangle (which exists whenever 180 ∉ θℤ: isoceles (ε, ε, 180 − 2ε) avoiding a finite excluded set), Shan-Yu survives forever — the necessity direction of the problem.
